column1: Copied from the latest tailored AI Readiness content pack.

column4: recommendation_summary
column5: recommendation_detail

column4: Define a business-led AI thesis before discussing tools
column5: Run an executive alignment exercise that names the business problems AI should address, the outcomes it should move, the workflows that matter first, and the sponsor accountable for decisions.

column4: Formalise the AI portfolio, ranking logic, and ownership model
column5: Create a standing use case intake and prioritisation method covering value, feasibility, data dependency, risk, and ownership so the best pilot candidates rise to the top consistently.

column4: Translate strategy into a governed pilot portfolio
column5: Convert the approved AI strategy into a small pilot portfolio with sequencing, named sponsors, stage gates, and business-value expectations for each use case.

column4: Map the real workflows before selecting AI interventions
column5: Document the candidate workflows end to end, isolate the actual failure points, and distinguish process redesign and automation needs from true AI opportunities.

column4: Tighten workflow design for shortlisted AI use cases
column5: Strengthen workflow boundaries, pain-point definition, review points, and suitability logic so the shortlisted use cases are pilot-ready rather than conceptually promising only.

column4: Advance the best-fit workflows into pilot design
column5: Take the most stable, high-value workflows and build pilot-ready operating designs including triggers, user touchpoints, review steps, and expected outcomes.

column4: Build the minimum viable data foundation for the selected pilots
column5: Identify the exact data inputs each pilot needs, clean critical fields, organise supporting knowledge, and assign ownership so pilot outputs are grounded in usable data.

column4: Improve data consistency, access, and ownership around priority use cases
column5: Standardise key fields, tighten data quality controls, improve access to the supporting data and knowledge assets, and close the biggest ownership gaps.

column4: Package approved data and knowledge inputs for controlled pilot use
column5: Curate the structured datasets and knowledge assets that will feed the pilots, including freshness rules, ownership, and controls for ongoing maintenance.

column4: Define a realistic embed-and-integrate path before deployment
column5: Map where data enters, where AI outputs should land, which systems need to connect, and which manual handoffs must be removed for the selected workflows to work in practice.

column4: Strengthen workflow embedding and orchestration for pilots
column5: Design cleaner insertion points, reduce manual transfers, and clarify dependency and support ownership so pilot outputs can sit inside the live workflow reliably.

column4: Use the existing technology base to embed AI into operational tools
column5: Implement the pilots inside the systems of work already used by teams, rather than launching a disconnected side workflow.

column4: Stabilise the process and automate rules before adding AI
column5: Resolve core process instability, close SOP gaps, handle obvious automation candidates, and reduce dependence on tribal workarounds before AI is introduced.

column4: Tighten process control in the selected workflows
column5: Clarify business rules, exception handling, handoffs, and oversight design so AI operates inside a cleaner and more consistent process.

column4: Introduce AI first where process maturity is already strongest
column5: Start with the most repeatable workflows and use those pilots to prove value before expanding into more variable or exception-heavy processes.

column4: Put AI operating controls in place before any material rollout
column5: Define approved data-use rules, sensitive-data restrictions, review requirements, vendor checks, and governance ownership for the selected AI scenarios.

column4: Convert policy into working control mechanisms for pilots
column5: Build the review matrix, audit trail expectations, access controls, and risk review process needed to govern real pilot workflows rather than principle only.

column4: Embed governance into pilot delivery and scale decisions
column5: Use the existing control foundation to govern approvals, exceptions, incidents, and evidence-based scale decisions across the AI portfolio.

column4: Build adoption readiness before launch
column5: Strengthen leadership understanding, assign adoption ownership, address resistance drivers, and prepare the affected teams for how work and oversight will change.

column4: Formalise training, manager enablement, and change support
column5: Create role-based enablement for managers, users, reviewers, and support teams so pilot adoption is actively managed rather than assumed.

column4: Use the existing change capacity to scale adoption deliberately
column5: Expand from early pilots through manager alignment, champions, feedback loops, and operating-model reinforcement rather than one-off communications.

column4: Define value, baseline, and success criteria before further investment
column5: Create a simple but credible value model for each priority use case, define the baseline, and specify what measurable success must look like before approving rollout.

column4: Standardise pilot measurement and scale decision rules
column5: Build pilot scorecards, pre/post comparison logic, and explicit thresholds for scale, redesign, or stop decisions so evidence drives the next step.

column4: Turn measurement into portfolio governance
column5: Use the existing KPI and ROI capability to review live AI performance on a regular cadence and make disciplined investment decisions across the portfolio.
